ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic omelette class; no specific onion omelette subclass exists. Mapped to closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ covers omelette as a generic egg dish; onion variant not separately coded. Used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for onion omelette as a food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of onion omelette is approximately 180 g (one plate), providing around 423 kcal, 15.7 g protein, and 35.8 g fat.

nutrient_source.title: Onion Omelette — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for onion omelette (cr_id F01050008). Secondary nutrients (sugar, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) were present in the input record attributed to a delivery-platform source; these have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for egg omelette (FDC ID 172185) and retained where consistent. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — egg-based dishes and omelette analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for onion omelette in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 49 (low) based on the closest analogous egg-based dish (plain omelette / scrambled eggs) from the University of Sydney GI Database, which reports egg dishes in the GI range 40–55 due to negligible starch content and high protein content slowing gastric emptying. Carbohydrate per serving = 5.2 g × 1.8 (180 g serving) / 100 = 9.36 g. GL = GI × carbohydrate_per_serving / 100 = 49 × 9.36 / 100 ≈ 4.6 (low GL).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 477 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100 g and omit soy sauce and added salt entirely, as sodium at 477 mg/100 g significantly contributes to daily sodium load; preparing the omelette with herbs and pepper instead of salt can reduce sodium by approximately 30–40%.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate at 5.2 g/100 g with only 2.0 g sugar and high protein at 8.7 g/100 g supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Onion omelette is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content (5.2 g/100 g) and high protein (8.7 g/100 g), which slows gastric emptying and supports postprandial blood glucose control; avoid adding sweet condiments such as ketchup or sweet chilli sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High cholesterol at 275 mg/100 g and elevated sodium at 477 mg/100 g; saturated fat at 4.0 g/100 g is moderate
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume onion omelette in moderation (no more than 1–2 times per week), use minimal cooking oil, and omit added salt and soy sauce to manage the combined burden of dietary cholesterol (275 mg/100 g) and sodium (477 mg/100 g); the saturated fat content of 4.0 g/100 g is within acceptable limits if portion size is controlled.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 477 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg RA threshold; absence of omega-3 or strong anti-inflammatory factors
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish as prepared due to high sodium (477 mg/100 g) which may worsen tissue swelling; reducing salt and omitting soy sauce, and pairing with omega-3-rich foods or anti-inflammatory spices such as turmeric, would improve suitability.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Onion omelette is a simple, widely consumed egg-based dish made by beating whole eggs and pan-frying them with sliced or diced onion in cooking oil, then folding the cooked egg into a flat or half-moon shape. It is a staple across Singaporean home cooking and hawker culture, commonly served as a side dish alongside steamed rice or as part of a mixed-plate meal. The texture is soft and slightly golden on the outside with a tender interior; the onion adds mild sweetness and a slight crunch. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at 235 kcal/100 g, driven primarily by fat (19.9 g/100 g) from cooking oil and egg yolk. Protein content is substantial at 8.7 g/100 g, making it a meaningful protein source. Carbohydrate is very low at 5.2 g/100 g, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns and Type 2 Diabetes management. Sodium is elevated at 477 mg/100 g due to added salt and optional soy sauce, which is a concern for hypertension and rheumatoid arthritis. Cholesterol is high at 275 mg/100 g, reflecting the egg yolk content. The dish is naturally gluten-free, halal, and vegetarian. It is not suitable for vegans or those with egg allergies. Calcium (52 mg/100 g) and iron (2.0 mg/100 g) contribute modestly to micronutrient intake. Vitamin C (2.5 mg/100 g) is present from the onion. The glycemic index is estimated as low (approximately 49) based on analogous egg-based dishes, with a glycemic load of approximately 4.6 per 180 g serving.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F01050008, confidence 0.95). Secondary nutrients cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for egg omelette. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous egg-dish category. All mandatory multilingual names provided for Singaporean cuisine category.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, potassium, and extended micronutrient data for onion omelette are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for egg omelette (FDC ID 172185) at the level of this specific preparation as of 2026. Sugar (2.0 g/100 g), cholesterol (275 mg/100 g), saturated fat (4.0 g/100 g), trans fat (0.1 g/100 g), and vitamin C (2.5 mg/100 g) were present in the input record attributed to a delivery-platform source; these have been retained after cross-validation against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy values for egg omelette, which are consistent. The input record contained two duplicate portion entries both labelled '1 plate' with different weights (180 g and 200 g); 180 g has been adopted as the default serving size as the more conservative estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database egg-dish category; no direct onion omelette GI entry exists in any approved GI source.
